title: The tool-use contract: you define, Claude decides, you execute

body.concept: Tool use is a typed contract between your application and Claude. You define the schema (what operations exist, what inputs they accept, what outputs they produce). Claude decides when and how to call them based on the conversation. Your code executes the operation and returns results. Claude never runs anything — it emits a structured request. This makes tool use feel like integrating any typed API: define the interface, handle the callback, return a result. The only difference is the caller is a language model choosing which function to invoke.
body.analogy: Like a restaurant menu. You (the chef) define the menu (tool schemas). The customer (Claude) reads the menu and places an order (tool_use). The kitchen (your app) prepares the dish (executes the tool). The waiter brings back the plate (tool_result). The customer never enters the kitchen.
body.visual: Schema definition → Claude selects tool + builds args → Your app executes → Result returned to Claude. Three roles: you (schema author + executor), Claude (decision maker), result (the bridge back).
